title: About
text:
Hello! I am Francesco D’Amico, a PhD student in Physics at Sapienza University of Rome, where I work under the supervision of Matteo Negri and Chiara Cammarota on the topic of statistical theory of learning.
My research lies at the intersection of statistical mechanics, machine learning, and complex systems.

title: About
text:
Research Interests

Data structure in machine learning and neural scaling laws
Associative memories and attention-class architectures

Approximate likelihood methods in energy-based models

Optimization and inference in hard computational tasks

title: Projects
text:
Bachelor degree

Excellence program

Theoretical study of Green method and numerical applications to real problems
Green method to solve a one-dimensional oscillator with a general force applied 🔽 pdf
Green method to solve the Laplacian equation of electric potential given a general charge distribution 🔽 pdf
Montecarlo methods for statistical mecanics
My first statistical mechanics numerical study: I analyzed the phase transition in 2-D Ising model 🔽 numerical phase transition



Thesis

Modello di Vicsek topologico analizzato con tecniche di Machine Learning (Topological Vicsek model analyzed with Machine Learning methods). I performed parallel dynamics on GPUs of flock of birds and I used a DGCNN model to infer hidden parameters of simulations. 🔽 Thesis

title: Publications
text:
Below is a complete list of my publications.




References

Algorithmic thresholds in combinatorial optimization depend on the time scalingAngelini, M., Avila-González, M., D’Amico, F., Machado, D., Mulet, R., & Ricci-Tersenghi, F.2025. arXiv preprint arXiv:2504.11174TL;DR: We studied the scaling behaviour with training time of algorithmic thresholds of simulated Annealing algorithm in k-SAT and Q-col problems.


Implicit bias produces neural scaling laws in learning curves, from perceptrons to deep networksD’Amico, F., Bocchi, D., & Negri, M.2025. arXiv preprint arXiv:2505.13230TL;DR: We show with a simple Perceptron theory how the norm of a model can be used as a useful parameter to predict its scaling laws. We show with extensive simulations that also deep-networks in real datasets classification show the same behavior.


Pseudo-likelihood produces associative memories able to generalize, even for asymmetric couplingsD’Amico, F., Bocchi, D., Del Bono, L. M., Rossi, S., & Negri, M.2025. arXiv preprint arXiv:2507.05147TL;DR: We studied pseudo-likelihood training in an analitically tractable model. We show why it behaves as an associative memory and that during training time it interpolates between already known learning rules. We show that such model is capable of generalization and that it works even if couplings are not symmetric.


Statistical mechanics of vector hopfield network near and above saturationNicoletti, F., D’Amico, F., & Negri, M.2025. arXiv preprint arXiv:2507.02586TL;DR: We studied the Vector Hopfield network with Hebb couplings in arbitrary number of dimensions, computing the equilibrium phase diagram and discovering an out-of-equilibrium denoising behaviour absent in standard hopfield model.


Self-attention as an attractor network: Transient memories without backpropagationD’Amico, F., & Negri, M.2024. 2024 IEEE workshop on complexity in engineering (COMPENG), 1–6TL;DR: We show that a simplified self-attention model trained directly via pseudo-likelihood minimization behaves as an associative memory at the very first step of dynamics.
